quarters.summary: Reliance Industries Ltd. reported a strong Q1 FY26-27 performance driven by oil prices, Jio, and Retail, while managing macro volatility and supply chain challenges. The company is focusing on digital scale in Retail and maintaining operational resilience in Energy/O2C despite geopolitical uncertainties.

subsections.label: Consolidated performance and what drove it
points: Management framed the quarter as 'extraordinary' due to macro volatility, energy market shock, and supply chain dislocation.
Revenue increased by 25% YoY, primarily driven by oil prices, with Jio and Retail also contributing.
EBITDA exceeded Rs. 54,000 Cr, a 10% increase, and PAT was approximately Rs. 23,200 Cr, a 6% increase.
Consumer businesses now constitute about 50% of the overall mix.
Capex was funded by cash profit, with net debt slightly lower QoQ at approximately Rs. 1.23 lakh Cr.
Q1 capex was around Rs. 39,000 Cr.
Moody's upgraded the rating to Baa1 and S&P to A-.
Finance cost increased by 19% and depreciation by 9% due to Jio capitalization.

subsections.label: Oil-to-Chemicals (Refining & Marketing + Petrochem)
points: O2C revenue increased by 30% YoY, with EBITDA up 17% to approximately Rs. 17,000 Cr.
Headline cracks/deltas overstate realizable economics due to shock costs, including premiums close to $20 a barrel, freight rate increases, and insurance costs.
RIL maintained 96–97% throughput despite a logistics/availability crisis, while the rest of Asia had runs down 15% to 20%.
Middle East production fell by about 12 million bpd during this period.
RIL diversified crude sources and optimized trade/logistics to meet contractual commitments.
SAED drag and domestic under-recoveries were headwinds, along with planned turnaround and LPG diversion under government direction.
High cracks were observed, but demand destruction occurred outside India.

subsections.label: Petrochemicals
points: Quarter described as a roller coaster with crude changing 5% to 10% in a day.
Naphtha increased by 61% to $903/ton, with premiums and freight spikes pushing regional crackers to under-run.
Middle East PE supplies were blocked, leading to PE delta surges, while PP remained firm and PVC delta fell by ~10%.
Polyester deltas rose despite MEG delta declining, with China constrained by crude availability.
RIL's structural edge was its ethane advantage, with 70% of ethylene feed being ethane, cheaper than naphtha.
Ethane logistics improved with new VLECs, increasing ethane availability.

subsections.label: Exploration & Production
points: Revenue increased by 3.2% YoY, with EBITDA almost flat/slightly lower.
KGD6 production decline was lower than expected, while CBM production crossed 1 million standard cubic meters of gas.
Condensate realizations were high at ~$107/bbl.
Rig arriving next month for KG basin exploration, with a multi-year, multi-well campaign planned.
CBM continues with a 40-well program and multilaterals across both blocks.
Gas prices remain elevated, with KGD6 ceiling expected to rise to ~$9.9 in 2H.

subsections.label: Jio Platforms
points: JPL revenue was Rs. 39,173 Cr, with EBITDA at Rs. 20,865 Cr and a margin of 53.3%.
Subscribers reached 533m, with 5G users at 285m and home broadband at 28.6m.
ARPU was Rs. 215.6, with digital services revenue growth outpacing connectivity.
Enterprise strategy includes managed services-led bundling with security, managed Wi-Fi, ERP, analytics, and surveillance.
FWA leadership with 78% market share of net additions in India.
PAT at JPL was Rs. 764 Cr, with finance cost higher due to interest expensing post-5G build capitalization.

subsections.label: Reliance Retail
points: Q1 revenue was approximately Rs. 90,000 Cr, with underlying growth of 11.6%.
Operating metrics showed a digital mix shift, with customers up 11% and transactions up 46%.
Grocery digital orders increased by 116% YoY, with digital share accelerating.
EBITDA was Rs. 6,309 Cr, with a margin of 7.9%, slightly down YoY.
Three-year roadmap includes laying the foundation and scaling with discipline, leveraging scale benefits into margins and cash generation.
Quick commerce differentiation claims include proprietary transaction data and physical footprint leverage.

subsections.label: RCPL (FMCG)
points: Revenue was Rs. 8,600 Cr, with staples/daily essentials at Rs. 3,200 Cr and beverages at Rs. 2,900 Cr.
Distribution includes 5,000+ distributors and 3m+ retail outlets, with presence in 40+ international markets.
Beverages claim #3 NARTD player in India, with plans to enter Australia and Africa next quarter.
Manufacturing includes commissioning one of the largest beverage plants in Asia and exploring an edible oil facility in West Bengal.
M&A/brand integration includes majority stake in Sosyo JV and operational transition for Toni & Guy, Brylcreem, Badedas, Matey.

subsections.label: JioStar (Media)
points: Platform crossed 530m users, with IPL reach of 700m and CTV consumption up 19%.
Product/tech initiatives include Tadka micro-content hub and deep integration with OpenAI for conversational discovery.
JAMS in-house AI media studio launched the first fully AI-generated micro-content.
Commerce integration with Swiggy enables transactions within the content stream.
Revenue was Rs. 10,946 Cr, with PBT also up 14%.
Headwinds include advertising challenges due to real-money gaming ban and US-Iran war impact.

subsections.label: Digital services and enterprise strategy
points: Digital services revenue growth was approximately 20% YoY, outpacing connectivity.
Management sees scope for far more monetization, with margins expected to improve with scale and operating leverage.
Enterprise push is managed services-led, bundling connectivity with security, managed Wi-Fi, ERP, analytics, and surveillance.
FWA leadership with 78% market share of net additions in India.
Patent position includes around 4,500 patents across the full network stack.

subsections.label: Quick commerce differentiation claims
points: Proprietary advantage from transaction data over the last 20 years and a loyalty program.
Physical footprint leverage with presence in over a thousand markets in grocery and dark store within a store concept.
Supplier terms enable lower consumer prices while maintaining margin.
Dark stores expansion continues for at least the next 12 months, with scale back where unit economics don't hold.

subsections.label: Strategic optionality
points: LEO constellation with 1,600 satellites is complementary, with investment planned when economics are proven.
Overseas opportunity details are constrained to DRHP.
Leadership changes described as routine, with Pankaj Pawar elevated and KT focusing on AI initiatives.
Meta partnership includes a 168 MW Jamnagar data center project providing end-to-end services, with funding currently on RIL's balance sheet.

subsections.label: RCPL (FMCG)
points: M&A/brand integration includes majority stake in Sosyo JV and operational transition for Toni & Guy, Brylcreem, Badedas, Matey.
Commenced sales in UK/Europe/Australia, with India launch preparation underway.

subsections.label: Headwinds called out explicitly
points: SAED drag and domestic under-recoveries, including sales to PSUs.
Planned turnaround lowered production meant for sale by 10%.
LPG diversion under government direction impacted petchem feed slate.
Advertising hit by real-money gaming ban and US-Iran war impact on the ad market.

subsections.label: Forward view
points: Management maintains a constructive tone but contingent on geopolitical factors.
Structural support for cracks from lost capacity in the Middle East and Russia.
Management refused near-term profitability calls due to renewed SOH volatility.
KGD6 ceiling expected to rise to ~$9.9 in 2H.

guidance_ledger.statement: KGD6 ceiling expected to rise to ~$9.9 in 2H.
Continued strong growth in digital services expected.
Continued expansion of retail network and services.
Progress in new energy projects and green ammonia supply contracts.
Commissioning of the first fully integrated 10-gigawatt peak annual solar manufacturing gigafactory is on track.
Battery manufacturing is progressing with a 40-gigawatt hour capacity, expanding to 100-gigawatt hour.
On track in terms of both the panel production and development of RERTC from sometime next year.

risk_register.note: Including sales to PSUs.
Lowered production meant for sale by 10%.
Impacted petchem feed slate.
Due to real-money gaming ban and US-Iran war impact.
Supply shock and its impact on industry and consumer confidence.
11% depreciation for the year and 4% in March.
Middle East conflict led to supply disruptions and high costs.
Natural decline is less than anticipated, working on augmenting production.

risk_register.risk: Adverse weather conditions impacting CBM production
risk_register.note: Weather conditions caused roadblocks in drilling multilateral wells.
Due to geopolitical tensions and OPEC Plus actions.

risk_register.risk: Weak demand and overcapacities in global Naphtha cracker operating rates
risk_register.note: Operating rates remain weak due to demand and overcapacities.

themes.label: Crude price volatility
themes.note: Management highlights extreme crack expansions but notes demand destruction outside India.

themes.label: Segment growth
themes.note: Jio and Retail segments showed strong revenue growth, contributing to overall performance.

themes.label: AI development
themes.note: JioStar's AI-led product shifts and deep integration with OpenAI are strategic initiatives.

themes.label: Oil & Gas production
themes.note: KGD6 decline managed with CBM as a growth lever; gas prices remain elevated.

themes.label: Digital services growth
themes.note: Digital services revenue growth outpaced connectivity, with scope for further monetization.

key_takeaways: RIL is explicitly trading near-term consumer margins for digital scale (Retail) and building new infrastructure moats (dark stores; data centers; AI/media tooling).

In Energy/O2C, management stresses realizable margin is constrained by procurement/logistics friction even when market cracks are extreme; nonetheless, RIL demonstrated operational resilience (near-full runs) and feedstock advantage (ethane-heavy slate).

Forward commentary is deliberately non-committal on near-term O2C earnings due to geopolitical volatility ('a lot of imponderables'), but management maintains confidence in structural positioning: 'structurally… we are in a good place right through.'

quarters.summary: Reliance Industries Ltd anticipates continued growth across its segments, with a focus on expanding its digital services, retail, and new energy initiatives, despite challenges in the oil and gas sector due to geopolitical tensions.

subsections.label: Group Performance
points: Revenue up 10% for FY2025-26, EBITDA up 13.5%, driven by consumer businesses contributing over 55% of EBITDA.
PAT growth was strong, with RIL at 24%, JPL at 15%, and RRVL at 12%.
Digital services showed strong growth with an 18% increase, while retail grew by 8% on EBITDA.
Oil-to-Chemicals segment faced challenges with a 4% decline in Q4 due to supply chain issues and geopolitical tensions.

subsections.label: Jio Platforms
points: Jio Platforms ended the year with 524 million subscribers, adding 36.3 million during the year.
5G user base reached 268 million, making it the largest outside China.
Revenue for the year was Rs.1,46,085 Crores, up 14.6% year-on-year.
EBITDA was Rs.76,255 Crores, with a margin of 52%.

subsections.label: Retail
points: Retail business achieved highest ever revenues of Rs.98,000 Crores in Q4, with 11% Y-o-Y growth.
Opened 333 new stores during the quarter, crossing 20,000 stores milestone.
Grocery and fashion segments showed strong double-digit growth.
JioMart serves 1200+ cities with 3100 stores, offering two-hour delivery.

subsections.label: FMCG
points: FMCG revenue reached Rs.22,000 Crores in 2026, doubling from the previous year.
Campa brand became the fourth largest carbonated soft drink brand with Rs.4700 Crores revenue.
Independence brand delivered Rs.2600 Crores revenue, becoming a trusted brand.
Package drinking water business became the third largest in the country.

subsections.label: JioStar
points: JioStar achieved 550 million monthly active users in March, driven by sports and entertainment content.
Broke a world record for concurrent streams during the T20 World Cup with 72.5 million streams.
Launched AI-driven technologies like Tadka micro-content and ChatGPT integration for enhanced user experience.

subsections.label: Jio Platforms Technology
points: Implemented AI-first network for energy optimization and customer experience improvements.
Developed proprietary beam form cell design to enhance coverage and capacity in high traffic zones.
Launched network innovations for improved customer experience in specific locations.

subsections.label: JioStar Technology
points: Launched Tadka micro-content and integrated ChatGPT for enhanced search functionality.
Introduced in-app commerce integration with Swiggy for seamless food ordering during live events.

subsections.label: FMCG Manufacturing
points: Investing in manufacturing capabilities to become one of the largest cooldrink manufacturers in India.
Operating 12 plants across India, with plans for further expansion as the business grows.

subsections.label: Geopolitical Tensions
points: Supply shock and its impact on industry and consumer confidence due to geopolitical tensions.
Rupee depreciation of 11% for the year and 4% in March, raising concerns about economic stability.

subsections.label: Oil & Gas Sector Challenges
points: Middle East conflict led to supply disruptions, impacting crude availability and refining operations.
High freight and insurance costs due to geopolitical tensions, affecting margins.

subsections.label: Future Outlook
points: Continued focus on expanding digital services, retail, and new energy initiatives.
Anticipation of strong growth in digital services and retail segments despite challenges in oil and gas.

guidance_ledger.statement: Continued strong growth in digital services expected.
Continued expansion of retail network and services.
Progress in new energy projects and green ammonia supply contracts.
Commissioning of the first fully integrated 10-gigawatt peak annual solar manufacturing gigafactory is on track.
Battery manufacturing is progressing with a 40-gigawatt hour capacity, expanding to 100-gigawatt hour.
On track in terms of both the panel production and development of RERTC from sometime next year.

risk_register.note: Supply shock and its impact on industry and consumer confidence.
11% depreciation for the year and 4% in March.
Middle East conflict led to supply disruptions and high costs.
Natural decline is less than anticipated, working on augmenting production.

risk_register.risk: Adverse weather conditions impacting CBM production
risk_register.note: Weather conditions caused roadblocks in drilling multilateral wells.
Due to geopolitical tensions and OPEC Plus actions.

risk_register.risk: Weak demand and overcapacities in global Naphtha cracker operating rates
risk_register.note: Operating rates remain weak due to demand and overcapacities.

themes.label: Segment growth
themes.note: Strong growth in digital services and retail segments.

themes.label: New Energy initiatives
themes.note: Progress in new energy projects and green ammonia supply contracts.

themes.label: AI development
themes.note: Implementation of AI-first network and new AI-driven technologies.

themes.label: Oil & Gas production
themes.note: Challenges due to geopolitical tensions and supply disruptions.

themes.label: Crude price volatility
themes.note: High freight and insurance costs affecting margins.

key_takeaways: Strong growth in digital services and retail segments.
Challenges in oil and gas sector due to geopolitical tensions.
Continued focus on expanding new energy initiatives.
pressing_questions: How much of distillate yield can actually change due to alternate crudes?
What is the level at which price increases are considered for fuel retail?
How is the situation in Q1 regarding procurement, freight, and other costs?
What percentage of production is impacted by SAED?
What are the opportunities in network slicing for telecom?

quarters.summary: Reliance Industries Ltd continues to show strong growth across its diversified businesses, with significant progress in new energy initiatives and robust performance in digital services and retail.

subsections.label: Group Performance
points: Revenue is up 10%, EBITDA up about 6%, and PAT at 22,290 crores, which was also up about 1.6%.
Revenue has been led by digital services and retail, with EBITDA driven by strong O2C performance, 15% higher.
Balance sheet remains steady with no change in net debt number, leverage, etc., and businesses continue to deliver cash.
S&P changed the rating from BBB Plus to A Minus, focusing on higher earnings from less cyclical businesses.

subsections.label: Retail
points: Retail business achieved the highest ever revenue at 97,600 Crores, up 8.1%.
EBITDA came in at 6,915 Crores with a margin of 8%.
Hyperlocal commerce is scaling rapidly with 1.6 million orders, showing 53% growth quarter-on-quarter.
Entered into an exclusive partnership with Fabletics, an American women's athleisure brand.

subsections.label: Jio Platforms
points: Jio Platforms ended the quarter with 515 million subscribers, adding 8.9 million net adds.
5G user base is growing rapidly with 253 million plus 5G subscribers.
Revenues crossed 37,000 Crores with an EBITDA margin of 52%.
Jio has 65% share of 5G subscribers in India.

subsections.label: FMCG
points: RCPL became a direct subsidiary of RIL from 1st December 2025, adding 5,000 Crores as revenue.
Daily essentials category grew 1.5 times year-on-year.
Energy portfolio reached 1,000 Crores in the quarter.
Four brands have crossed 1,000 Crores plus by Q3.

subsections.label: O2C - Petrochemicals
points: Ethane prices were up 21% due to stronger US Henry Hub gas prices.
Naphtha prices declined by 14% due to weaker crude prices.
Polyethylene and polypropylene saw good growth of 4% and 8% respectively.
PVC demand reduced by 12% due to prolonged monsoon.

subsections.label: Jio Platforms Technology
points: Developed proprietary technologies across the network, with 4G and 5G running on in-house stack.
World's largest fixed wireless operator with in-house developed solutions.
Home OS and tele OS offered to homes on connected devices, scaled to over 25 million homes.

subsections.label: New Energy Manufacturing
points: Commissioning of the first fully integrated 10-gigawatt peak annual solar manufacturing gigafactory is on track.
Solar module manufacturing has reached a high yield of 94%-95%.
Battery manufacturing is progressing with a 40-gigawatt hour capacity, expanding to 100-gigawatt hour.

subsections.label: New Energy
points: Progress on the new energy ecosystem is well on track, with significant advancements in solar and battery manufacturing.

guidance_ledger.statement: Commissioning of the first fully integrated 10-gigawatt peak annual solar manufacturing gigafactory is on track.
Battery manufacturing is progressing with a 40-gigawatt hour capacity, expanding to 100-gigawatt hour.
On track in terms of both the panel production and development of RERTC from sometime next year.

risk_register.note: Natural decline is less than anticipated, working on augmenting production.

risk_register.risk: Adverse weather conditions impacting CBM production
risk_register.note: Weather conditions caused roadblocks in drilling multilateral wells.
Due to geopolitical tensions and OPEC Plus actions.

risk_register.risk: Weak demand and overcapacities in global Naphtha cracker operating rates
risk_register.note: Operating rates remain weak due to demand and overcapacities.

themes.label: Segment growth
themes.note: Strong growth across digital services, retail, and energy segments.

themes.label: New Energy initiatives
themes.note: Significant progress in solar and battery manufacturing capacity.

themes.label: AI development
themes.note: AI-enabled smart factories in new energy manufacturing.

themes.label: Oil & Gas production
themes.note: Efforts to manage decline in KG D6 fields and augment production.

themes.label: Crude price volatility
themes.note: Naphtha prices declined due to weaker crude prices, impacting margins.

key_takeaways: Customer addition strong at about 9 million, taking the total to about 515 million customers.
Retail revenue growth has been about 8%, and overall EBITDA growth has been 1.3%.
Quick commerce is progressing with a 1.6 million orders run rate.
FMCG turnover is more than 5,000 Crores, up 60% year-on-year.
Energy business shows strong growth, 15%, with transportation fuel growth between 60% to 100% year-on-year.
Jio-bp shows 24% growth in volumes for both gas oil and gasoline.
Revenue is up 10%, EBITDA up about 6%, and overall PAT at 22,290 crores, up about 1.6%.
Digital service up 16%, with strong O2C performance, 15% higher.
Balance sheet remains steady with no change in net debt number, leverage, etc.
S&P changed the rating from BBB Plus to A Minus.

quarters.summary: Reliance Industries Ltd continues to demonstrate strong performance across its diverse business segments, with significant growth in Jio Platforms, Retail, and New Energy initiatives, while maintaining a stable financial position.

subsections.label: Group Performance
points: Strong performance across businesses with improving quality of numbers.
EBITDA margin expansion observed.
Overall EBITDA growth at about 15% higher on a set of big numbers.
PAT at 22,100 Crores, up 14% despite higher finance costs and depreciation.
Net debt broadly flat with CAPEX close to 40,000 Crores.

subsections.label: Jio Platforms
points: Operating as a next-gen technology company at scale with proprietary 5G stack.
506 million subscribers with 8.3 million net additions during the quarter.
21 million net additions in 5G users this quarter.
36,000 Crores plus revenue with 52% EBITDA margin for JPL.

subsections.label: Retail
points: 18% growth on a YOY basis with strong performance across consumption baskets.
Added about 400 plus new stores during the quarter.
Quick Commerce offering scaling up aggressively.
Gross revenue up 18%, net revenue up 19%.

subsections.label: Media & Entertainment
points: 400 million MAUs with a sharp jump in EBITDA.
Big Boss and Special Ops series performed exceedingly well.
JioHotstar became the largest sports platform with significant viewership growth.

subsections.label: Oil & Gas
points: Slightly better EBITDA quarter-on-quarter despite natural decline in KG-D6 fields.
Deep-water rig to augment production from KG-D6 fields.
Price realization higher year on year with ceiling price at $9.72.

subsections.label: Refining & Marketing
points: Revenue up 3.2%, EBITDA up 20.9%, margin up 130 basis points.
Maximized crude throughput and optimized yield for higher profitability.
Strong performance of Jio-bp in domestic market with significant growth in diesel and petrol sales.

subsections.label: Petrochemicals
points: US ethane prices up 47% compared to last year, but ethane cracking remains favorable.
Reliance Cracker operating rates at 100% supported by domestic demand.
Polyethylene and polypropylene demand grew by 4% and 9% respectively.

subsections.label: Jio Platforms Technology
points: Proprietary 5G stack and fixed wireless stack operating at global scale.
3400 plus patent applications across 5G and 6G.
Developing end-to-end control over the value chain for cost efficiency.

subsections.label: AI Initiatives
points: Reliance Intelligence formed as a 100% subsidiary to develop AI capabilities.
Partnership with Meta for enterprise solutions.
Developing AI for consumers and enterprises with a focus on infrastructure and products.

subsections.label: New Energy Manufacturing
points: Building end-to-end integrated manufacturing ecosystem for solar and battery.
Solar cell gigafactories starting up next month at Jamnagar.
Battery energy storage system gigafactories to start by early next year.

subsections.label: Oil & Gas Risks
points: Natural decline in KG-D6 fields, but less than anticipated.
Adverse weather conditions impacting CBM production.

subsections.label: Market Risks
points: Volatile crude prices due to geopolitical tensions and OPEC Plus actions.
Weak demand and overcapacities in global Naphtha cracker operating rates.

subsections.label: Future Outlook
points: Focus on brand building, bottling capacity, and setting up food parks post NCLT approval.
On track for panel production and development of RERTC from next year.
Expecting to ramp up the rate of connecting new homes significantly.

guidance_ledger.statement: On track in terms of both the panel production and development of RERTC from sometime next year.

risk_register.note: Natural decline is less than anticipated, working on augmenting production.

risk_register.risk: Adverse weather conditions impacting CBM production
risk_register.note: Weather conditions caused roadblocks in drilling multilateral wells.
Due to geopolitical tensions and OPEC Plus actions.

risk_register.risk: Weak demand and overcapacities in global Naphtha cracker operating rates
risk_register.note: Operating rates remain weak due to demand and overcapacities.

themes.label: Segment growth
themes.note: Strong performance across Jio Platforms, Retail, and Media segments.

themes.label: New Energy initiatives
themes.note: Progressing with integrated manufacturing ecosystem for solar and battery.

themes.label: AI development
themes.note: Reliance Intelligence formed to develop AI capabilities and partnerships.

themes.label: Oil & Gas production
themes.note: Working on augmenting production despite natural decline in fields.

themes.label: Crude price volatility
themes.note: Volatile due to geopolitical tensions and OPEC Plus actions.

key_takeaways: Strong performance across businesses with improving quality of numbers.
Jio Platforms achieved 18% growth in EBITDA with significant subscriber additions.
Retail segment delivered 18% YOY growth with expansion in store footprint and quick commerce.
Media segment maintained 400 million MAUs with strong content performance.
Oil & Gas segment working on augmenting production despite natural decline in fields.
Refining & Marketing segment saw significant growth in EBITDA due to higher fuel cracks.
New Energy initiatives progressing with integrated manufacturing ecosystem for solar and battery.
pressing_questions: First question to Anshuman on the tariff side. We have seen in the last quarter some nudges as you mentioned. Now going forward should we expect that more or given that we are now six quarters since a base tariff hike in the near term something on the base tariff as well as what we saw last year.

And then on homes. We are now consistently hitting 1 million plus. So, are we close to the peak in terms of monthly run rate? Because if we do that, then we are reaching to our 100 million ambition closer to 7 years from now. Or should we expect this rate to also increase in the coming few quarters?

On the AI bit you explained a lot of the entities will be a 100% owned subsidiary and the go-to vehicle could be something like Jio. Now from a group perspective, if I look at the value proposition, I mean what could be at the IP level and what could be the value proposition by the vehicle like Jio, how should we think?

Now Reliance Intelligence is all about DC or GPU as a service, will it reside in Jio or it will be in Reliance Intelligence and as far as DC to DC connectivity, on the connectivity part will it go to the InvIT or the Reliance Jio will have any role to play in that?
Any commercial development to talk about there? Are we close to signing any tech deal globally? Any commerciality there you can share?
Any broad understanding in terms of capex investments we could see that over a period of time? And can you clarify if the DC investments will be a part of Reliance Intelligence or not?

In the presentation on corporate side there was a mention about rural enterprises. Can you clarify these are offerings also towards SMEs or we are yet to launch a corporate package focusing on the SMEs?

quarters.summary: Infosys anticipates a challenging FY27 with a revenue growth guidance of 1.5%-3.5% CC YoY, factoring in macroeconomic headwinds and strategic decisions to not pursue certain deals. The company remains focused on AI-driven growth and maintaining stable margins despite expected wage hikes and productivity pass-throughs.

subsections.label: Margins
points: FY26 operating margin was 21%; Q4 was 20.9%. Gains from currency and Project Maximus were reinvested in talent, AI, and sales and marketing.

subsections.label: Cash & Working Capital
points: DSO was 78, the lowest in 7 years. FY26 FCF was $3.5bn (adjusted), with FCF conversion at 106% for the year and 111% for Q4.

subsections.label: Capital Return
points: Returned over $4 bn to shareholders in FY26; proposed final dividend of INR 25, total INR 48 (+11.6% YoY).

subsections.label: Large Deals
points: FY26 TCV for large deals was $14.9–$15.0bn, a 28% YoY increase, with 55% net new. Q4 TCV was $3.2bn across 19 large deals. Management emphasized the strength of the large deal pipeline.

subsections.label: Vertical/Geography Mix
points: Outperformance in Financial Services, Communications, Manufacturing, and Europe. Communications, Manufacturing, and Europe grew more than double the company average, driven by large deal wins.

subsections.label: Growth Quality/Realization
points: Volumes for the year were flattish; growth was led by an increase in realization due to Project Maximus.

subsections.label: AI Strategy
points: Infosys reiterated its AI thesis from Investor Day, highlighting a large addressable market for AI services across six areas. Core platforms include Topaz Fabric (AI) and Cobalt (cloud).

subsections.label: Proof Points
points: Ralph Lauren's AI tool increased revenue by 12% and customer engagement by 50%. Hertz migrated 3 mn lines of COBOL code to microservices, reducing cost by 60% and timeline by 60%. BP's 50 AI agent initiatives improved payment accuracy by 95%, contract validation speed by 50%, and IT operations efficiency by 18%.

subsections.label: AI Revenue Disclosure
points: AI contribution is above the prior disclosed 5.5%, but management withheld updated percentages.

subsections.label: Vertical Operating Signals
points: Financial Services grew 4.4% in FY26, driven by large-deal ramps, AI-led transformation, legacy modernization, and vendor consolidation. Market sentiment remains positive with expected budget growth in US banking and capital markets.

subsections.label: Acquisitions/JV in Guidance
points: Stratus (insurance-related) is included in guidance, contributing ~25 bps to FY27 growth. Optimum Healthcare and Versent JV are excluded as they are pending regulatory approvals.

subsections.label: Risk Flags and Notable Management Stances
points: Geopolitics (Middle East/Iran war) acknowledged as changing the economic environment, but management sees paths towards stabilization. Competitive intensity and 'irrational' bids noted, with Infosys walking away from deals not aligned with ROIC expectations. AI security/vulnerability angle highlighted as a services opportunity.

subsections.label: FY27 Outlook
points: Revenue guidance is 1.5%–3.5% CC YoY. The lower end assumes higher deterioration in the environment, while the upper end assumes improvement. Operating margin guidance is 20%–22%. Known headwinds include a 0.75%–1% revenue reduction from a large European manufacturing client and an expected onsite mix reduction of 0.75%–1%.

guidance_ledger.statement: Revenue guidance is 1.5%–3.5% CC YoY.
Operating margin guidance is 20%–22%.
0.75%–1% revenue reduction due to lower revenue from one of our large European manufacturing clients.
Expected additional onsite mix reduction of 0.75%–1%.
The opportunity is between $300 bn and $400 bn in the year getting to 2030.

risk_register.note: Management acknowledged a change in economic environment after conflict escalation, but sees paths towards stabilization.
Management acknowledged occasional competitor proposals outside the range of what models can deliver.
Framed as a services opportunity to help clients protect vulnerabilities.
Security breaches are increasing, necessitating modernization of legacy systems to enhance security.
The deployment gap between AI technology and enterprise capacity to use it is a significant challenge.
The results for Q3 FY'26 include a charge associated with change in labor codes in India, impacting operating profit, net profit, EPS and free cash flow.
Retail and CPG clients continue to experience uncertainty due to the ongoing tariff negotiations and evolving geopolitical equations.
Infosys has reduced its dependence on H-1B visas by increasing local hiring and establishing nearshore centers.

themes.label: AI Deployment
themes.note: Infosys is leveraging AI to drive growth, with significant contributions from AI projects.

themes.label: Strategic Partnerships
themes.note: New partnerships with OpenAI, Anthropic, and others enhance Infosys' AI capabilities.

themes.label: Security Challenges
themes.note: AI security vulnerabilities present both challenges and opportunities for Infosys.

themes.label: AI Services Growth
themes.note: AI services are a key growth driver, with contributions exceeding previous disclosures.

themes.label: Client Engagement
themes.note: Infosys is expanding its footprint with net new large deals and AI-first projects.

key_takeaways: Revenue growth: FY26 +3.1% CC, Q4 +4.1% CC YoY; Q4 -1.3% CC QoQ driven by “seasonality and slower decision making in the month of March.”
Scale milestone: FY26 revenues “crossed $20 bn.”

Vertical/geography mix: Outperformance called out in Financial Services (FS), Communications, Manufacturing, and Europe (CEO); CFO added Communications, Manufacturing and Europe grew more than double the company average, led by “ramp-up of the large deal wins.”
Large deals: $14.9–$15.0bn FY26 TCV (mgmt uses both), +28% YoY, 55% net new; Q4 $3.2bn, 19 large deals. Management emphasized “Large deal pipeline continues to remain strong.”
Growth quality/realization: “Volumes for the year were flattish, growth was led by increase in realization thanks to Project Maximus,” i.e., pricing/realization rather than volume.
Margins: FY26 operating margin 21%; Q4 20.9%. Company stressed reinvestment: “Gains from currency and Maximus were reinvested in talent, AI and sales and marketing.”
Cash & working capital: DSO 78 (lowest in 7 years); FY26 FCF $3.5bn (adjusted), FCF conversion 106% (FY) / 111% (Q4).
Capital return: Returned “over $4 bn to shareholders in FY26”; proposed final dividend INR 25, total INR 48 (+11.6% YoY).

Talent: End headcount ~328k; voluntary attrition 12.6% (down 1.5%). Q4 headcount down ~8k sequentially; CFO framed as demand/utilization-driven with seasonality; FY headcount still +5k YoY. Fresher intake: >20k in FY26, expects ~20k in FY27 again.
FY27 outlook: guidance mechanics and what is embedded
Revenue guidance: 1.5%–3.5% CC YoY (2-pt range). CFO: lower end assumes “higher deterioration in the environment,” upper end assumes “improved environment.”
Seasonality view: CEO: “We expect H1 to be stronger than H2 consistent with our normal seasonality.”
Operating margin guidance: 20%–22%.
Acquisitions/JV in guidance:

Included: Stratus (insurance-related; already closed). CFO quantified it as ~25 bps contribution to FY27 growth in press Q&A; on earnings call he reiterates guidance “includes contribution from Stratus.”
Excluded (not yet closed): Optimum Healthcare and Versent JV (pending regulatory approvals).
Known headwind explicitly baked into FY27 guidance:

0.75%–1% revenue reduction due to “lower revenue from one of our large European manufacturing client,” combining (i) reduced spend in “challenging macro environment” and (ii) Infosys’ “conscious decision to not pursue certain deals that were not aligned to our return expectations.”
Further guidance drags from delivery mix: expected additional onsite mix reduction of 0.75%–1%; third-party costs expected to be similar to FY26 levels.

Why guidance range narrower than last year: CFO said last year’s wider range reflected abrupt shifts near guidance time (tariffs uncertainty); now there is “a better clarity… the environment has been like this for last few quarters and we know how clients are behaving.”
AI strategy: positioning, monetization signals, and where AI is cutting vs creating revenue
The strategic frame
Infosys reiterated its AI thesis from Investor Day: a “large addressable market for AI services” across six areas: AI strategy & engineering, data, process, legacy modernization, physical AI, trust.
Core platforms: Topaz Fabric (AI) and Cobalt (cloud) as differentiators “operational today.”
Proof points: production-scale examples (not pilots)
Management explicitly pushed back on “pilot-only” narratives:
CEO: “There are like a number of projects on production, like large-scale products… There is not a pilot at all.”
Examples provided on the earnings call:
Ralph Lauren: conversational/personalized AI tool; “increase in their revenue by 12% and customer engagement by 50%.”
Hertz: migrated “3 mn lines of COBOL code” to microservices using foundation models; “cost was 60% lower, the timeline was 60% quicker.”
BP: “50 AI agent initiatives” across multiple functions; outcomes cited: “95% payment accuracy, 50% faster contract validation and 18% improvement in IT operations efficiency.”
Partner ecosystem and internal rollout
New and recent partnerships: OpenAI (announced “yesterday” vs the call), Anthropic; also listed Google Gemini, NVIDIA, Microsoft, AWS, Google Cloud, Intel.
Tooling adoption: 30,000+ developers on GitHub Copilot.

Workforce enablement: management stated 90%+ of employees have been “skilled on different types of the AI platforms”; fresher program now includes tiered compensation for AI-aligned skills and a “forward deployed engineer” construct for client-facing AI build work.
AI revenue disclosure: growing, but management withheld updated %s
They confirmed AI contribution is above the prior disclosed 5.5% (Q3), with CEO stating “Yes” when asked if higher, but refused updated numbers: “we are not giving the number.”
Investors should note deliberate disclosure tightening: management said they shared 5.5% at Investor Day to show materiality and growth; “At one stage we will, but today we are not sharing it.”
Where AI is deflating spend vs creating new spend
Management described a two-sided ledger: “compression is coming on some of the services and the growth is coming on other services.”
Compression shows up where foundation models/tooling are “very efficient,” cited specifically across some tech services and some BPM.
Productivity pass-through is explicitly acknowledged as margin headwind: CFO: “competitive intensity… the productivity will get passed back to the client largely.”

Importantly, management claims the growth engine (AI-first work + net new large deals + footprint expansion) currently exceeds compression: CEO said “the growth part… is larger today than the compression,” supporting positive net growth guidance.
Vertical operating signals (demand, budgets, and deal motifs)
Financial Services (highlighted as FY27 accelerator)
CFO: FS FY26 grew 4.4%; driven by large-deal ramps, AI-led transformation, legacy modernization, and vendor consolidation.
Market tone: “Overall market sentiment remains positive,” with “continued consumer spending across US banking, capital markets and Europe” and “CY26 budgets are expected to grow in US.”
Competitive positioning: “strategic AI partner for 18 out of the top 20 clients.”
Notable deal archetype: signed “a large GCC deal for a regional bank in the US… and a large AI-first GCC deal.”
EURS (Energy/Utilities/Resources/Services): constructive, net-new heavy
CFO: demand “constructive supported by a strong large deal pipeline,” with vendor consolidation driven by cost takeout.
Energy: “increased outsourcing leading to healthy deal momentum.”
Utilities: structurally higher demand due to “grid constraints, renewables integration and acceleration electricity needs for a data center.”
Key forward indicator: “80% of the large deal TCV of FY26 was net new,” positioned as a driver of FY27 acceleration.
Manufacturing: mixed—macro caution + one large client headwind
CFO: “cautious amid softer demand particularly in automotive and parts of Europe,” plus “tariffs and ongoing Middle East conflict” causing delayed decisions.
Spend mix: discretionary constrained; focus on cost optimization and operational resilience.
Pipeline themes: infrastructure outsourcing, AMS, S/4HANA rollouts.
Quantified FY27 drag: “Near term and FY27 growth will be impacted due to low revenue from one large client” (the same European manufacturing client headwind described in guidance).
Retail & Communications: budget pressure, AI for efficiency
Retail: budgets “tightly controlled,” discretionary under pressure; clients want AI productivity to “do more with the similar budgets”; expects demand for AI-assisted legacy modernization.

Communications: budgets “flat to negative,” discretionary impacted; shift “from Generative to Agentic AI,” with clients consolidating IT+BPM to cut costs; uptick in AI deals in IT ops, software replacement, mainframe migration.
Margin, pricing, and delivery model: what’s changing
Pricing: CFO said pricing environment “remained stable,” and notably “most of our growth this year has been pricing-led” due to softer volumes; also said AI revenue comes at “better pricing.”
AI margin profile: CFO: AI projects typically have better pricing and margin, but also premium talent cost; advantage depends on being “ahead of the curve.”
Q4 margin walk (key drivers):
Headwinds: 50 bps amortization (past acquisition), 30 bps normalization of Q3 one-off, 20 bps comp-related.
Offsets: 40 bps currency, 30 bps Project Maximus.
FY27 margin headwinds explicitly named: “wage hikes, productivity pass-throughs and AI investment,” offset by Maximus.
Acquisition/JV margin impact: post-closure annualized margin impact for Optimum + Stratus + Versent combined estimated at ~0.7%.

Onsite mix: continued structural shift down (Q4 onsite mix 22.8%, down from 23.1%). CFO attributes to environment, visa situations, and internal offshore delivery push; also noted discretionary declines tend to reduce onsite needs.
Risk flags and notable management stances

Geopolitics (Middle East/Iran war): management acknowledged a change in economic environment after conflict escalation, but sees “paths towards things stabilizing”; still ties macro stability to tech spend recovery.

Competitive intensity & “irrational” bids: Management acknowledged occasional competitor proposals “outside the range” of what models can deliver; Infosys may walk away (explicitly done in the European manufacturing case for ROIC discipline).

AI security/vulnerability angle (Mythos): CEO described Mythos as “exposing more vulnerabilities than one thought possible,” framing it as a services opportunity to help clients “make sure that your vulnerabilities are better and quickly protected” (early-stage discussions).
Leadership/succession questions: CEO gave “No comment” on tenure/succession-related queries.

quarters.summary: Infosys is focusing on AI-driven transformation across various sectors, emphasizing modernization, talent transformation, and strategic partnerships to leverage AI opportunities.

subsections.label: AI Transition
points: AI transition is faster than previous tech shifts, reaching 1 billion users in a couple of years compared to 10 years for the Internet and 5 years for smartphones.
AI is a fundamental change in business operations, not just a layer of technology.
Modernization of legacy systems is crucial to leverage AI effectively.
AI speed is facilitated by existing ubiquitous infrastructure like the Internet and smartphones.

subsections.label: AI Strategy and Engineering
points: Infosys is engaged in AI strategy work, building agents, and orchestrating platforms.
AI strategy involves creating an AI blueprint and transformation office, focusing on unlocking AI value in specific business units.

subsections.label: AI Augmented Services
points: Infosys is infusing AI into traditional services like application development, testing, and modernization.
Partnerships with leading AI models and tools are crucial for delivering AI-augmented services.
Infosys has created agents for specific services and developed enterprise context to enhance AI tools.

subsections.label: AI-first Services
points: Infosys has identified six growth areas for AI services: AI strategy and engineering, data for AI, process reimagination, legacy modernization, physical AI, and AI trust.
AI-first services involve creating new roles and reskilling talent to handle AI-driven tasks.

subsections.label: Client Engagement
points: Infosys is trusted by clients for driving and delivering AI work, with AI projects for 90% of its large 200 clients.
The AI-first value framework is used to engage clients, focusing on revenue growth, cost optimization, and innovation.

subsections.label: AI Demand in Communications, Media, and Tech
points: AI is becoming a core operating model for telecom, media, and tech companies, driving customer experience, engineering, and operational efficiency.
AI is helping telecom companies reimagine customer journeys and go-to-market strategies.

subsections.label: Strategic Partnerships
points: Infosys is working with AI native partners and universities for joint research and innovation.
Partnerships with companies like Telstra and Microsoft are crucial for expanding AI services and capabilities.

subsections.label: Security and Modernization Challenges
points: Security breaches are increasing, necessitating modernization of legacy systems to enhance security.
The deployment gap between AI technology and enterprise capacity to use it is a significant challenge.

subsections.label: AI Services Growth
points: Infosys sees a large opportunity in AI services, with an estimated market size of $300 billion to $400 billion by 2030.
The focus is on unlocking AI value for clients and driving growth through AI-first and AI-augmented services.

guidance_ledger.statement: The opportunity is between $300 bn and $400 bn in the year getting to 2030.
The new revenue growth guidance for this financial year is 3% to 3.5% growth in constant currency.
Our operating margin guidance for the financial year remains the same at 20% to 22%.

risk_register.note: Security breaches are increasing, necessitating modernization of legacy systems to enhance security.
The deployment gap between AI technology and enterprise capacity to use it is a significant challenge.
The results for Q3 FY'26 include a charge associated with change in labor codes in India, impacting operating profit, net profit, EPS and free cash flow.
Retail and CPG clients continue to experience uncertainty due to the ongoing tariff negotiations and evolving geopolitical equations.
Infosys has reduced its dependence on H-1B visas by increasing local hiring and establishing nearshore centers.

themes.label: Client Engagement

key_takeaways: AI transition is faster than previous tech shifts, reaching 1 billion users in a couple of years compared to 10 years for the Internet and 5 years for smartphones.
AI is a fundamental change in business operations, not just a layer of technology.
Modernization of legacy systems is crucial to leverage AI effectively.
AI speed is facilitated by existing ubiquitous infrastructure like the Internet and smartphones.
Infosys is trusted by clients for driving and delivering AI work, with AI projects for 90% of its large 200 clients.
The AI-first value framework is used to engage clients, focusing on revenue growth, cost optimization, and innovation.
AI is becoming a core operating model for telecom, media, and tech companies, driving customer experience, engineering, and operational efficiency.
AI is helping telecom companies reimagine customer journeys and go-to-market strategies.
Infosys is working with AI native partners and universities for joint research and innovation.
Partnerships with companies like Telstra and Microsoft are crucial for expanding AI services and capabilities.
Security breaches are increasing, necessitating modernization of legacy systems to enhance security.
The deployment gap between AI technology and enterprise capacity to use it is a significant challenge.
Infosys sees a large opportunity in AI services, with an estimated market size of $300 billion to $400 billion by 2030.
The focus is on unlocking AI value for clients and driving growth through AI-first and AI-augmented services.

quarters.summary: Infosys has revised its revenue growth guidance upwards to 3% to 3.5% for FY26, driven by strong large deal wins and AI adoption across its client base. The company remains focused on expanding its AI capabilities and strategic partnerships to capture market share in emerging AI-led value pools.

subsections.label: Margin Analysis
points: Adjusted operating margins increased by 20 basis points sequentially to 21.2%.
Operating margins including the impact of change in labor codes stands at 18.4%, adjusting for the same, it is at 21.2%.
The puts and takes of 20 basis point expansion this quarter is 40 basis points came from currency, 50 basis points came from Project Maximus, offset by the furloughs and lower working days.

subsections.label: Cash Flow and Capital Allocation
points: Free cash flow generation adjusted for labor codes remained robust at $965 mn, which is 113% of adjusted net profit.
During the quarter, Infosys successfully completed its largest ever buyback, returning INR18,000 crores to shareholders.

subsections.label: Large Deals
points: Large deal TCV was robust at $4.8 bn in Q3 with 57% net new.
Total large deal TCV for 9 months stood at $11.7 bn, exceeding the total large deal TCV of full year FY'25.
Net new deal TCV for 9 months was up by 40% as compared to the same period last year.
Large deal pipeline continues to be healthy.

subsections.label: Financial Services
points: Momentum in Financial Services continues with 3.9% year-on-year growth in constant currency terms.
We are now a preferred AI partner for top 15 out of 25 banking clients.
Uptick in discretionary spend in aforementioned sub-verticals and deal wins in recent quarters positions us favorably for better growth in FY'27.

subsections.label: Geographical Performance
points: Among geos, Europe continued to lead the growth by 7.2% year-on-year in constant currency terms.

subsections.label: Utilization and Headcount
points: Utilization, excluding trainees was down by 1% sequentially at 84.1% and including trainees, was down by 2.2% to 80% as we continue to create capacity for future growth opportunities.
Net headcount increased by 5,000 to 337,000 employees.
LTM attrition declined by 2% sequentially and 1.4% on a year-on-year basis, reflecting both market conditions and our focus on employee retention and upskilling.

key_figures.label: Lines of Code Generated Using AI

subsections.label: AI Deployment
points: We have deepened our Topaz AI capability with an agent services suite called Topaz Fabric.
We are currently working on 4,600 AI projects.
Our teams have generated over 28 mn lines of code using AI.
We have built over 500 agents.
We are scaling our forward deployed engineer team.
We are witnessing six AI-led value pools emerging that could unlock a large incremental opportunity.

subsections.label: Strategic Partnerships
points: We are expanding our strategic partnerships with AI companies.
We recently announced a partnership with Cognition. Here, we will combine Cognition's Devin software agent with Infosys' knowledge of the client landscape and industry expertise.
We have a good pipeline of possible companies that we are looking at and discussions.

subsections.label: Labor Code Impact
points: The results for Q3 FY'26 include a charge associated with change in labor codes in India, which had an impact on operating profit, net profit, EPS and free cash flow for the quarter.
There will be an ongoing impact of roughly around 15 basis points.

subsections.label: Geopolitical and Regulatory Risks
points: Retail and CPG clients continue to experience uncertainty due to the ongoing tariff negotiations and evolving geopolitical equations.
Communication sector continues to be impacted by geopolitical uncertainty.

guidance_ledger.statement: The new revenue growth guidance for this financial year is 3% to 3.5% growth in constant currency.
Our operating margin guidance for the financial year remains the same at 20% to 22%.

risk_register.note: The results for Q3 FY'26 include a charge associated with change in labor codes in India, impacting operating profit, net profit, EPS and free cash flow.
Retail and CPG clients continue to experience uncertainty due to the ongoing tariff negotiations and evolving geopolitical equations.
Infosys has reduced its dependence on H-1B visas by increasing local hiring and establishing nearshore centers.

themes.label: AI Deployment
themes.note: Infosys is expanding its AI capabilities with Topaz Fabric and strategic partnerships, working on 4,600 AI projects.

themes.label: Revenue Growth Guidance
themes.note: Infosys raised its revenue growth guidance to 3% to 3.5% for FY26 due to strong large deal wins.

themes.label: Large Deal Activity
themes.note: Large deal TCV was robust at $4.8 billion in Q3 with 57% net new.

themes.label: Labor Code Impact
themes.note: The change in labor codes in India impacted operating profit, net profit, EPS and free cash flow.

themes.label: Strategic Partnerships
themes.note: Infosys is expanding strategic partnerships with AI companies, including a partnership with Cognition.

key_takeaways: Infosys has revised its revenue growth guidance upwards to 3% to 3.5% for FY26, driven by strong large deal wins and AI adoption across its client base.
The company reported a sequential revenue growth of 0.6% and a year-on-year growth of 1.7% in constant currency terms for Q3 FY26.
Large deal TCV was robust at $4.8 billion in Q3 with 57% net new, and the total large deal TCV for 9 months stood at $11.7 billion, exceeding the total large deal TCV of full year FY'25.
Infosys has deepened its Topaz AI capability with an agent services suite called Topaz Fabric, and is currently working on 4,600 AI projects.
The company has expanded its strategic partnerships with AI companies, including a recent partnership with Cognition.
The results for Q3 FY'26 include a charge associated with change in labor codes in India, impacting operating profit, net profit, EPS and free cash flow.
Infosys completed its largest ever buyback, returning INR18,000 crores to shareholders.
pressing_questions: How much of the revenue growth guidance upgrade is due to large deals like NHS being factored in?
What is the outlook for next year now that Infosys is completing the 20,000 fresher hires for the year?
How will the wage hikes impact margins from here on?
What are the pricing models evolving with the newer AI technologies?
What is the impact of the labor code changes on profit margins and appraisals going forward?

quarters.summary: Infosys has increased its revenue growth guidance for FY26 to 2-3% in constant currency terms, maintaining operating margin guidance at 20-22%. The company is focusing on AI deployment across enterprises and continues to scale its team of forward-deployed engineers.

subsections.label: Quarterly Performance
points: Revenues for the quarter grew 2.2% sequentially and 2.9% year-on-year in constant currency terms.
Operating margin was 21%.
Free cash flow stood at $1.1 billion, which is 131% of the net profit.

subsections.label: Cash Flow and Investments
points: Consolidated cash and investments were at $6.2 billion at the end of the quarter.
Yield on cash balance was at 6.98% and ROE stood at 29.1%.

key_figures.label: Net New Work in Large Deals

subsections.label: Deal Activity
points: Large deals were at $3.1 billion, with 67% net new work.
A mega deal worth $1.6 billion was announced after the close of the quarter.

subsections.label: Industry Performance
points: Financial Services and Manufacturing grew above 5% year-on-year in constant currency.
Europe also grew greater than 5% year-on-year in constant currency terms.

subsections.label: AI Initiatives
points: Infosys is delivering more than 2,500 Generative AI and AI projects and 200-plus agentic AI projects for clients.
The company is scaling its team of forward-deployed engineers to support AI deployment across enterprises.

subsections.label: Regional Performance
points: Europe has been a strong performer, with significant investments in different countries.
The U.S. market remains a focus for growth and investment.

subsections.label: Acquisitions and Joint Ventures
points: Infosys announced a joint venture with Versent in Australia, pending regulatory approvals.
The company is actively looking at other acquisition opportunities.

subsections.label: H-1B Visa Issue
points: Infosys has reduced its dependence on H-1B visas by increasing local hiring and establishing nearshore centers.
The company does not share specific numbers on employees dependent on H-1B visas.

subsections.label: Guidance Update
points: Revenue growth guidance for the financial year is 2% to 3% in constant currency terms.
Operating margin guidance remains at 20% to 22% for the full year.

guidance_ledger.statement: The new guidance is growth between 2% and 3% in constant currency terms for the full year.
Our operating margin guidance remains the same as in the past quarter at 20% to 22% for the full year.

risk_register.note: Infosys has reduced its dependence on H-1B visas by increasing local hiring and establishing nearshore centers.

themes.label: AI Deployment
themes.note: Infosys is focusing on deploying AI across enterprises for growth and cost efficiency.

themes.label: Revenue Growth Guidance
themes.note: Revenue growth guidance for FY26 has been raised to 2-3% in constant currency terms.

themes.label: H-1B Visa Strategy
themes.note: Infosys has reduced its dependence on H-1B visas by increasing local hiring and establishing nearshore centers.

themes.label: Large Deal Activity
themes.note: Large deals totaled $3.1 billion, with 67% net new work, and a mega deal worth $1.6 billion was announced post-quarter.

themes.label: Segment Performance
themes.note: Financial Services and Manufacturing grew above 5% year-on-year in constant currency.

key_takeaways: Infosys has increased its revenue growth guidance for FY26 to 2-3% in constant currency terms, maintaining operating margin guidance at 20-22%.
The company reported a strong Q2 performance with a 2.2% sequential revenue growth and a 21% operating margin.
Large deals totaled $3.1 billion, with 67% net new work, and a mega deal worth $1.6 billion was announced post-quarter.
Infosys is focusing on AI deployment across enterprises and continues to scale its team of forward-deployed engineers.
The company has reduced its dependence on H-1B visas by increasing local hiring and establishing nearshore centers.
pressing_questions: Are you not seeing a meaningful recovery, especially when it comes to your Manufacturing, Retail, these kind of verticals?
Could you give us in percentage terms, how many of your employees that you were sending abroad were on these visas?
What kind of contribution to revenue do you expect from the Versent acquisition?
In terms of the AI talk that is going on, could you tell us if you would be looking at anything of this sort this year in the next quarter or fourth quarter?

Do you think it is viable to continue sending your employees on these visas? Are you going to participate in the next cycle or is there going to be a significant pullback even in this minority number that you currently have?
